description: A sleeping person and a fish in water both 'whunei'. Their bodies exchange gases with the surroundings; in an ordinary human scene, air enters and leaves the lungs. 'whesu' (blow) instead follows air sent along a course, which a quiet breath need not do.
articulatory_notes: Round the lips and raise the back of the tongue for unvoiced 'wh', letting air pass without vocal-fold vibration, then continue into rounded high-back 'u'. Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for stressed dental 'n', carry the voice through the nose, and move into middle-height front 'e'. Give final high-front 'i' its own syllable rather than gliding from 'e'.
sound_symbolism: Unvoiced 'wh' makes breath audible before any oral closure. The dental nasal and clean 'e.i' hiatus turn that airflow into two measured beats, a useful contour for breath entering bodily rhythm.
usage_notes: 'whunei' is normally intransitive. Its event noun is a breath, so 'ta whunei' counts one breath. Inhalation and exhalation are phases of the same act; a text can follow the air entering or leaving when that direction matters.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Breath awareness attends to each 'whunei' without requiring the practitioner to control it. The ordinary bodily verb keeps the meditation object observable.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: A deliberate breath can make room before a difficult reply. 'whunei' names that bodily act without promising what the speaker will say next.

semantic_domains.activity: breathing as a continuing bodily act
semantic_domains.nature: respiration among living organisms in their surrounding medium
semantic_domains.physical: respiration through exchange with the surrounding medium
semantic_domains.ritual: breath used as an observable object in meditation practice
